Alright, welcome back. We're cooking with gas here and we're just about ready to move into the heart and soul of straight line prospecting. But first, before I actually do that, let me quickly sum up what we've gone through so far and I'll do it in four simple steps. Step number one. In your prospecting, you're asking your prospects a series of strategically-prepared questions in a way that does not break rapport, hence below the sale, and you're looking to identify those buyers at heat and buyers in power in terms of these primary things. That they need your product, want your product, can be helped by your product, and can also afford your product and then the big one right is where their pain lies. So far as how that relates to your product, that's step number one. Now step two. As you're doing that, you're also looking to quickly identify those dreaded looking lose and also the mistakes as well, right? And again, you're not going to be mean to these looking loser mistakes. You're not going to be nasty, but you want to weed them out as quickly and elegantly as possible because here's the deal. Making full-blown presentations to them is literally the death of a salesperson. You get it? That's step two. Now, moving on to step number three. You're looking to begin the process of turning your buyers in power into buyers in heat by amplifying their pain. This wakes them up from that denial they've been in, right, which creates urgency and hence makes them much easier to close. And now lastly, step number four, you want to end your intelligence gathering session by executing a powerful straight line transition, which then takes you into the main body of your sales presentation where you'll begin the process of what? Building absolute certainty for all three 10s. And you'll do that on both a logical and an emotional level, which then sets you up to close anyone who's closed more. So with that, let's now move into the 10 rules of straight line prospecting, starting with rule number one, which is you are a sifter, not an alchemist. Let me explain what I mean by that. An alchemist is a person that tries to turn a base metal like lead into a valuable metal like gold, like in the medieval times, right, the dark ages. You have these alchemists, they were always trying to find the secret to take one element, a worthless element and turn it into a precious element. So what's the comparison here? Well, what would be a worthless element? A look you were a mistake is a worthless element and a precious element would be a buyer in power or a buyer in heat, right? Well, here's the deal. I want you to look at this as you would look at prospecting for gold. Let me get one of those movies you see with the old fashioned prospectors with the scraggly beard and the tin pan and they're standing by a stream, right? And he puts his pan in the stream and he's sifting through water, sifting through thousands of gallons, millions of gallons of water. He's sifting through it not because he expects that if he stands there long enough, the water will transform into gold. No, he knows that if he sits there long enough, eventually a nugget of gold that was in the water will be deposited in his pan. He's not trying to turn water into gold. It doesn't work. That's the same thing that happens when you try to take a look you were a mistake and turn them into a buyer in power or a buyer in heat. It doesn't work. Buyers are buyers and look you lose or look you lose. You get it. They don't transform into each other. So when you're prospecting, this is the big mistake that rookies make is they try to force everybody into those first two categories. They're just so anxious to see, I want to pitch everyone. Don't do that. I want you to look at this as you're sifting. I'm sifting through a list of people to find the right ones and eliminate the wrong ones. That's what a prospector does. You get it? So I've got to think of the old fashioned prospect of gold. Do that. But the point is you're a sifter, not an alchemist. An alchemist does magic and transforms one thing into another. It doesn't work. Okay? So that's lesson number one. Now it's one lesson number two, which is that you must ask for permission to ask questions. Let me explain what I mean here. If you make your opening presentation and then before you say anything in terms of asking permission to ask questions, start bombarding someone with questions. So tell me, how long you've been in the market for? Or how long are you looking for insurance? They're like, whoa, what the hell? By simply saying to someone, I'd like to ask you a couple of quick questions so I can best serve you so I don't waste your time by asking permission. Is that okay? And then using that magic word, so, which is called your justifier, you not only dramatically increase your compliance rate, meaning that people will say, sure, I'll answer your questions, but also you don't break rapport. If you just plow through that process without first asking for permission to ask questions, you'll come off as that interrogator, the cop, the policeman who's like asking you questions to uncover something wrong versus someone who kind of says, listen, I want to ask you a couple of quick questions so I can best serve you. That's the trusted friend, the family advisor, right? So it has that best interested heart. That's who you want to be. So the simple word we use there is S-O. So a couple of quick questions so I don't waste your time. A couple of quick questions so I can best serve you. You get it? Very, very simple. Now let me just very quickly tell you the power here of a justifier. You're always saying, Jordan, does that really matter? I mean, the word so, how much power can that really have? Let me tell you a lot of power. They did a study many years ago back in one of the universities in the United States. It was a famous study done for influence. And here's the story. They had this copy machine in a library, right? It was very crowded library. And they waited until there was four students waiting at this machine to make copies, right? And then what they did is they went up to the front of the line and then the mold, right, the person working as part of the study, right? And they went up to the front line and said, excuse me, can I please cut the line and guess what happened? 25% of the time, the four students that were already waiting said, yeah, go ahead, sure, cut the line. But 75% of the time they said, no, wait your turn, moron. Come on, who the hell do you think you are, right? 25% compliance rate. That was their baseline. So they went back now a few days later, different students, same process, waited until there was four people at the copy machine, right? They walked to the front of the line, except this time when they asked them if they can cut the line, they used a justify watch. They said to them, excuse me, can I please cut the line or else I'm going to miss class. I'm really in a rush. People said, yes, sure. Why not? 75% of the time. Imagine 300% increase in compliance because there was a reason why. So I don't miss my class. You get it? 300% just because of a certain language pattern, powerful stuff, right? It gets even better, right? They said to themselves, well, maybe if the person gave a stupid reason, they wouldn't really comply. It's probably because we use the reason that all students can empathize. Does it make sense? They're going to be late for class. They've all had that problem. They don't want you to get in trouble so people can relate. So why don't we change it up and we'll say something totally ridiculous. Like can I cut the line because I really need to? Like something that just doesn't make sense, but they used a justify. Went back a few days later, four people standing there, right? Moles up to the front and says, excuse me, can I please cut the line? I really need to. 35% of the students, they let them cut the line. Can you imagine? What they found out was that it didn't matter what the reason was, just that there was a reason. See what happens with a justifier is it bypasses the logical mind and goes straight to the gut. It's a snap emotional reaction that people make when you give them a reason, whatever it's a reason, so they don't think much about it. Now obviously as salesmen, we don't want to be stupid about this, right? So of course you want to use the correct words. So you would never just say, can I ask you some questions? I feel like asking you a question. You say, so I can best serve you so I can help you get what you need. So I don't waste your time. So obviously you would use the right words, but the point is justifiers are very powerful because they work on an unconscious level below the conscious mind, no logic involved. Now interestingly enough, they didn't do this, but I can promise you knowing what I know about language in the straight line, if they would have gone back another day and now added the word only, so I said, can I cut the line? I'm in a big rush and I really need to. It'll only take a second. I bet you that 10 or 15% would have said, sure, go do it because that's what we call a minimizer. And we'll talk about that later on, but the point is that's your second rule of straight line prospecting. You must ask permission to ask questions and you want to use a justifier every single time. And with that, let's stop now and take a short quiz. I want to make sure you lock in all the things you've just learned here. And then when you're done, I will meet you in the next lesson and we'll plow forward. I'll see you there.
